---
title: "English Language Institute (ELI) Analysis"
author: "Arianna Goodman"
date: "2026-04-26"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## Loading Data

```{r}
library(tidyverse)

# Creating the dataset for averages by level (across all 3 categories)

df_promotion <- data.frame(
  Lvl = c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60),
  Y2015 = c(94.85, 77.55, 85.82, 70.13, 72.90, 47.06),
  Y2016 = c(96.25, 71.99, 73.38, 68.92, 77.53, 60.48),
  Y2017 = c(93.10, 77.40, 82.37, 70.52, 72.05, 49.04),
  Y2018 = c(88.46, 76.92, 80.28, 82.72, 75.08, 46.01),
  Y2019 = c(57.14, 87.67, 83.33, 81.11, 75.57, 29.09),
  Y2020 = c(100.00, 89.55, 90.08, 88.78, 79.23, 33.62),
  Y2021 = c(100.00, 95.08, 92.50, 87.93, 89.09, 82.29),
  Y2022 = c(100.00, 83.33, 94.34, 81.91, 89.72, 82.29),
  Y2023 = c(100.00, 82.22, 89.90, 84.98, 82.70, 58.58),
  Y2024 = c(85.19, 95.35, 92.81, 78.48, 88.74, 55.31)
)

# Rotate data frame
df_long <- df_promotion %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("Y"), 
               names_to = "Year", 
               values_to = "Rate") %>%
  mutate(Year = as.numeric(gsub("Y", "", Year))) # Clean year to be numeric
```


## Summary Statistics

```{r}
summary_stats <- df_long %>%
  group_by(Lvl) %>%
  summarize(
    Mean_Rate = mean(Rate),
    SD_Rate = sd(Rate), # Measures volatility
    Min_Rate = min(Rate),
    Max_Rate = max(Rate)
  )
summary_stats
```


## Plot Overall Annual Promotion Rates

```{r}
ggplot(df_long, aes(x = Year, y = Rate, color = as.factor(Lvl))) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Annual Promotion Rates by Level (2015-2024)",
       x = "Year", y = "Promotion Rate (%)", color = "Level") +# Force breaks to be every 2 years: 2015, 2017, 2019, etc.
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2015, 2025, by = 2)) + 
  theme_minimal()
```


## Correlation Matrix Between Levels

```{r}

# Correlation matrix (requires wide format)
# 1. Create the data frame with a unique name
promo_df <- data.frame(
  Lvl = c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60),
  Y2015 = c(94.85, 77.55, 85.82, 70.13, 72.90, 47.06),
  Y2016 = c(96.25, 71.99, 73.38, 68.92, 77.53, 60.48),
  Y2017 = c(93.10, 77.40, 82.37, 70.52, 72.05, 49.04),
  Y2018 = c(88.46, 76.92, 80.28, 82.72, 75.08, 46.01),
  Y2019 = c(57.14, 87.67, 83.33, 81.11, 75.57, 29.09),
  Y2020 = c(100.00, 89.55, 90.08, 88.78, 79.23, 33.62),
  Y2021 = c(100.00, 95.08, 92.50, 87.93, 89.09, 82.29),
  Y2022 = c(100.00, 83.33, 94.34, 81.91, 89.72, 82.29),
  Y2023 = c(100.00, 82.22, 89.90, 84.98, 82.70, 58.58),
  Y2024 = c(85.19, 95.35, 92.81, 78.48, 88.74, 55.31)
)

# 2. Run the correlation (dropping the 'Lvl' column for math)
# t() transposes it so we correlate Levels, not Years
cor_matrix <- cor(t(promo_df[, -1])) 

# 3. Add labels so the matrix is readable
rownames(cor_matrix) <- promo_df$Lvl
colnames(cor_matrix) <- promo_df$Lvl

print(round(cor_matrix, 2))
```


## Disaggregated Data by Categories (GM, LS, RW)

```{r}
library(tidyverse)

# 1. Define the Years and Levels
years <- 2015:2024
lvls  <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60)

# 2. RW Data (6 levels x 10 years = 60 values)
rw_values <- c(
  96, 72, 84, 60, 72, 48, # 2015
  100, 66, 72, 52, 81, 61, # 2016
  100, 77, 81, 62, 77, 50, # 2017
  90, 87, 81, 82, 82, 75,  # 2018
  63, 94, 74, 75, 75, 31,  # 2019
  100, 80, 80, 87, 70, 56, # 2020
  100, 94, 97, 80, 92, 66, # 2021
  100, 86, 92, 78, 93, 77, # 2022
  100, 81, 92, 79, 93, 51, # 2023
  89, 61, 86, 65, 85, 58   # 2024
)

# 3. LS Data (60 values) - Note the NAs for Level 10 in 2021-2023
ls_values <- c(
  95, 79, 89, 72, 64, 31, # 2015
  100, 76, 70, 73, 72, 45, # 2016
  100, 83, 79, 77, 66, 32, # 2017
  78, 91, 69, 75, 69, 25,  # 2018
  36, 87, 79, 88, 64, 18,  # 2019
  100, 94, 93, 91, 69, 24, # 2020
  NA, 93, 92, 93, 79, 91,  # 2021 (Level 10 is NA)
  NA, 60, 93, 82, 89, 88,  # 2022
  NA, 76, 93, 88, 93, 53,  # 2023
  100, 100, 92, 90, 93, 53 # 2024
)

# 4. GM Data (60 values) - Note the NAs for Level 10 in 2021-2022
gm_values <- c(
  96, 82, 87, 82, 86, 78, # 2015
  86, 74, 77, 85, 78, 82, # 2016
  83, 78, 87, 74, 72, 65, # 2017
  89, 61, 83, 92, 73, 51, # 2018
  27, 82, 92, 80, 88, 39, # 2019
  100, 98, 66, 91, 96, 41,# 2020
  NA, 100, 88, 98, 93, 90,# 2021
  NA, 89, 92, 86, 89, 88, # 2022
  NA, 77, 91, 92, 74, 72, # 2023
  83, 97, 97, 92, 90, 61  # 2024
)

# Combine into one clean data frame
promo_df <- data.frame(
  Year = rep(years, each = 6, times = 3),
  Level = rep(lvls, times = 30),
  Category = rep(c("RW", "LS", "GM"), each = 60),
  Rate = c(rw_values, ls_values, gm_values)
)

# 1. Install and load necessary libraries
if(!require(zoo)) install.packages("zoo")
library(zoo)
library(dplyr)

# 2. Fill the NAs using linear interpolation
# We group by Category and Level to ensure logical filling
promo_df_clean <- promo_df %>%
  group_by(Category, Level) %>%
  mutate(Rate = na.approx(Rate, na.rm = FALSE)) %>%
  ungroup()

# 3. Re-run your best regression model
final_model <- lm(Rate ~ Level + Year + Category, data = promo_df_clean)

# 4. Check the new results
summary(final_model)
```

## Final Model

```{r}
# 1. Fill the NAs using linear interpolation
promo_df_clean <- promo_df %>%
  group_by(Category, Level) %>%
  mutate(Rate = na.approx(Rate, na.rm = FALSE)) %>%
  ungroup()

# 2. Exclude the identified outliers (Rows 145, 85, 90)
promo_df_filtered <- promo_df_clean %>% 
  slice(-c(85, 90, 145))

# 3. Re-run the regression model on the filtered data
final_model_v2 <- lm(Rate ~ Level + Year + Category, data = promo_df_filtered)

# 4. Check the updated results
summary(final_model_v2)

# Check the new Q-Q plot to see the improvement
plot(final_model_v2, which = 2)


# Final model findings

library(jtools)
data(promo_df_filtered)
fit <- lm(Rate ~ Level + Year + Category, data = promo_df_filtered)

summ(fit)
plot(final_model)
```


## Generate Predictions for 2026

```{r}
# 1. Create a "New Data" frame for the future years
# This generates a row for every Level and Category for 2025 and 2026
future_years <- expand.grid(
  Level = c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60),
  Year = c(2025, 2026),
  Category = c("RW", "LS", "GM")
)

# 2. Generate the predictions
# 'interval = "confidence"' adds lower and upper bounds to your forecast
predictions <- predict(final_model, newdata = future_years, interval = "confidence")

# 3. Combine them into a readable table
forecast_table <- cbind(future_years, predictions)

# 4. View the 2026 forecast specifically
print(forecast_table[forecast_table$Year == 2026, ])

```


## Plot Promotion Rates by Category

```{r}
library(ggplot2)

# Create the plot with facets for each Category
ggplot(promo_df_clean, aes(x = Year, y = Rate, color = as.factor(Level))) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  geom_point() +
  # This creates 3 separate plots side-by-side
  facet_wrap(~Category) + 
  labs(title = "Promotion Rate Trends by Category",
       subtitle = "Comparing RW, LS, and GM Performance (2015-2024)",
       x = "Year",
       y = "Promotion Rate (%)",
       color = "Level") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```
